explanation.text: The word “obsessively” suggests that the author studies the catalog with unusually intense attention. This establishes interest because his effort reveals a strong passion for beginning beekeeping.

After discovering the catalog, the author orders a beginner's kit, assembles and paints the hive, and selects a bee supplier. This sequence develops paragraph 1 because it describes how he chose equipment and built his hive.

The author's first colony dies because he does not yet know to protect the bees from cold rain by replacing the lid. His costly mistake demonstrates that beekeeping requires knowledge and skills learned through experience.
The author says that tasting his honey made all the setbacks and lessons worthwhile. This most clearly reveals enthusiasm because he values the result enough to persist through years of failure.

The first colony dies before producing honey, and the second lives through the year but produces “not a drop.” Therefore, the author is unable to harvest any honey during either of his first two years.

The author begins with intense interest, eagerly ordering supplies and styling his hive. His attitude becomes satisfaction because, after learning from failures, he harvests enough honey for himself and his friends.

The author's colonies fail at first and require years of practice, yet the eventual honey makes his setbacks worthwhile. These contrasting results support the central idea that beekeeping is challenging but rewarding.
Words such as “mean,” “leaden,” and “moaned” make the sea and sky seem hostile and ominous. This imagery creates a tone of threat and danger because it signals the approaching storm's power.
The rapid series of motion words repeats “slamming” and gives no pause between the boat's movements. This rhythm shows the storm's persistent force battering David and the Frog.
Even after a wall of water repeatedly knocks David off his feet, he never releases the helm and keeps rising to steer. This detail best shows his determined attitude toward surviving the storm.

David adjusts the helm and the Frog's angle while deliberately maintaining a speed of five to six knots in the storm. Those controlled, technical actions demonstrate the judgment of an experienced sailor.

The sailors identify David as the missing boy who was far beyond the search area and had been presumed dead. Their reaction emphasizes his perseverance because he has survived the storm and an extraordinary distance at sea.

The investigation of lions, parasites, poachers, and food supply organizes the article's explanation of the population decline. This idea belongs in a summary because the following sections test each possible cause.
Calling the Serengeti one of Earth's “natural wonders” compares it to something extraordinary and worthy of admiration. This word choice indicates that Dr. Strauss considers the area a marvel.
The researchers hope their education program and resulting changes will reverse the giraffe population's decline. In this context, “rebound” means to recover from that challenge.

The article follows researchers as they investigate four explanations for the Serengeti's shrinking giraffe population and identify two likely causes. Therefore, determining the reasons for population change is its central idea.

The author explains that plentiful food helps females remain healthy and produce many calves, while shortages lead to fewer births. This link emphasizes that food is essential to sustaining giraffe reproduction.

The research identifies poaching and poor food supply as likely causes of the decline, then uses education to help Tanzanians respond. This offers a new evidence-based viewpoint on supporting the giraffe population.

The growth of trees giraffes do not eat reduces available food, which contributes to fewer calves, while poaching reduces adult numbers. These effects show that a change in one part of an environment can affect other parts.

The British meet near the Darraghs and speak freely because they mistakenly believe the Quaker family is neutral. Since Lydia secretly supports the Americans, that situation makes it easier for her to assist the war effort.
Lydia has been ordered to stay in bed during a top-secret meeting, yet the muffled sounds compel her to listen. Her reaction suggests that she senses the officers are discussing something important.
Lydia leaves her bed and tiptoes toward a secret meeting despite the officers' orders. Eavesdropping could expose her as a spy, so these actions develop her willingness to risk her safety.
Lydia knows the planned attack threatens the American soldiers, including her son Charles. Concern for his life therefore helps motivate her journey to warn Captain Craig.

Lydia speaks softly and quietly because the warning must remain secret; discovery could lead to a charge of treason and death. The phrase reminds readers that danger surrounds every stage of her mission.

General Howe finds the American troops already armed with cannons, proving that someone warned them. That discovery leads to the later investigation because the British want to learn how their plan was exposed.

Paragraph 2 explains that the British speak freely because they believe the Quaker Darraghs are neutral. Even after the warning reaches Washington, the officer leaves without suspecting Lydia, so that mistaken belief remains.
